name: Roosevelt Sykes
abstract: Roosevelt Sykes (January 31, 1906 – July 17, 1983) was an American blues musician, also known as "the Honeydripper."

about_quotes.speaker: Charles Lloyd
about_quotes.evidence: I played with... what’s this guy’s name? Roosevelt Sykes. I played with all these guys, man these blues.
about_quotes.doc_title: Charles Lloyd — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Red Holloway
about_quotes.evidence: my first gig on the road was with Roosevelt Sykes for a year, 1948. I went all over the U.S. with him.
about_quotes.doc_title: Red Holloway interviewed by Monk Rowe and Michael Woods, Scottsdale, Arizona, March 4, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Harold Ousley
about_quotes.evidence: In fact I worked with Sunnyland and Memphis Slim, Roosevelt Sykes.
about_quotes.doc_title: Harold Ousley interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 12, 2001

about_quotes.speaker: Ernest "Punch" Miller
about_quotes.evidence: The interviewee discusses a different musician named Roosevelt (Tampa Red/Hudson Whittaker) and mentions 'Roosevelt Sykes' only in the interviewer's question as the source of information about another person ('Roosevelt Sykes told me about it'). The interviewee does not claim a personal relationship

about_quotes.speaker: Henry "Jack" Palmer
about_quotes.evidence: I come over there and got Roosevelt Sykes and brought him to Knight Street to Hunter's Place.

about_quotes.speaker: Ann Rabson
about_quotes.evidence: there’s a song called “Skin and Bones” that’s a Roosevelt Sykes tune
about_quotes.doc_title: Ann Rabson interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, September 21, 2007
